title: initialDelaySeconds is not respected and probes are executed
body:
There is a small inconsistency with the `initialDelaySeconds`. As defined in [Kubernetes docs]( https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/configure-liveness-readiness-startup-probes/#configure-probes), it is mentioned that the probes will be executed after the  `initialDelaySeconds` is elapsed. It is even more clear in the [examples of the documentation]( https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/configure-liveness-readiness-startup-probes/?utm_source=chatgpt.com#define-a-liveness-command) where it is mentioned that: `The initialDelaySeconds field tells the kubelet that it should wait 5 seconds before performing the first probe.` 

[Podman executes the probe immediately]( https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/20789) and ignores the results until the time defined in `initialDelaySeconds` is elapsed. 

So I am guessing it is by design, but was it on purpose to have this discrepancy from the specs of Kubernetes? 
Would it be of interest to fix this discrepency ?




How to test:

Create `health-check-pod.yaml` with

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: healthcheck-test
spec:
  containers:
  - name: tester
    image: docker.io/library/alpine:3.19
    command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
    args:
      - |
        ts=$(date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S");
        echo "Starting container at $ts" | tee /tmp/healthcheck.log;
        # Keep container alive
        sleep infinity
    livenessProbe:
      exec:
        command:
          - /bin/sh
          - -c
          - |
            ts=$(date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S");
            echo "Healthcheck at $ts" | tee -a /tmp/healthcheck.log;
            # Real check: just always succeed
            true
      initialDelaySeconds: 120
      periodSeconds: 5
    volumeMounts:
    - name: tmp
      mountPath: /tmp
  volumes:
  - name: tmp
    emptyDir: {}
```

Then run:

```sh
podman kube play --replace health-check-pod.yaml
```

Observe that healthchecks are performed already from the start, before the 120 seconds have passed:

```sh
podman exec -it healthcheck-test-tester tail -f /tmp/healthcheck.log
Starting container at 2025-12-04 10:18:47
Healthcheck at 2025-12-04 10:18:47
Healthcheck at 2025-12-04 10:18:53
Healthcheck at 2025-12-04 10:18:59

```

Check health state, it should be in "Starting" until initial delay is over:

```sh
podman inspect healthcheck-test-tester --format '{{ json .State.Health }}' | jq
{
  "Status": "starting",
  "FailingStreak": 0,
  "Log": null
}
```

Tested on Podman 5.7.0

comments.body: We don't do this right now because of our healthcheck architecture (we need to make the systemd timer as part of the command, and it starts running immediately), but the proposed move of healthchecks to Conmon with conmon v3 could allow arbitrary delays before start like this.
We'd still be executing `podman healthcheck run`, but we'd stop before the exec instead of after, which seems preferable. If you want to open a PR with that change, it seems acceptable.
